data.title: Notable analyst calls this week: Apple, GM and Comcast stocks among top picks
data.description:
The S&P500 (SP500) closed in the green, after the week saw earnings report from firms including Netflix and UnitedHealth, as well as brewing tension between  U.S. President Donald Trump and Federal Reserve chair Jerome Powell.

For the week, both Nasdaq (COMP:IND) and Dow (DJI) declined over 3%.

Wall Street had a slew of upgrades and downgrades from analysts. Here are some of the major calls for the week:

Apple in focus amid Trump's tariffs scenario

KeyBanc Capital Markets upgraded Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) to Sector Weight from Underweight, while Wedbush maintained its Outperform rating amid the Trump administration's temporary reprieve on tariffs for electronics such as smartphones.

KeyBanc analysts said the announcement of exception from tariffs on smartphones is probably the best-case scenario they could think of for Apple. However, the analysts added that they don't think Apple is fully "out of the woods" due to — growth expectations remain high looking to fiscal '26 and there is likely still a consumer spending pullback occurring.

Wedbush analyst Dan Ives and his team said they remain bullish on the long-term opportunity for Apple's flagship ecosystem and focus on its 1.5 billion iPhone and 2.4 billion iOS installed base and massive Services business for downside protection in this nervous market backdrop for tech investors.

J.P. Morgan kept its Overweight rating on Apple but lowered PT to $245 from $270.

Deutsche Bank sees problematic second half of 2025 for auto sector, downgrades GM

General Motors (NYSE:GM) was downgraded by Deutsche Bank to Hold from Buy and shaved 26% off its $58 price target, saying that the second half of 2025 will be much more problematic for the sector due to tariffs.

“Ultimately, we don’t expect OEMs to bear the full burden of these extra costs as consumers and dealers will take some of the hit and automakers also implement various mitigation strategies. On a net basis, we estimate Ford and GM could face a $4B to $7B headwind on annualized EBIT,” Deutsche Bank’s Edison Yu said.

Yu expects both Ford (NYSE:F) and General Motors will deliver solid results, but will withdraw full-year guidance as they implement tariff mitigation strategies.

PayPal, BILL Holdings downgraded on rising uncertainty in 'tariff-heavy world'

Payment company PayPal Holdings (NASDAQ:PYPL) was downgraded to Sell from Neutral by Seaport, citing rising uncertainty in a "tariff-heavy world".

"We think it's going to be a struggle for the company to meet the guidance," said the analyst Jeff Cantwell, adding, especially if the discretionary spend starts to slow.

Similarly, the brokerage downgraded BILL Holdings (NYSE:BILL) to Neutral from Buy, saying that it sees risk to the company’s prior guidance that it will accelerate core revenue growth to 20% in FY26 and that the numbers don't seem to be achievable in this evolving macro environment.

Comcast downgraded by Wells Fargo

Wells Fargo downgraded media and cable giant Comcast (NASDAQ:CMCSA) to Underweight from Equal weight, as the brokerage thinks the stock is no longer defensive as it is being pulled into a convergence investment cycle with higher mobile costs to re-accelerate broadband.

Wells Fargo cut PT for Comcast by $6 to $31, as they also see an impact on NBCU due to a weakened ad market, expect elevated sports costs for the NBA, and see no break-even at Peacock.

CFRA boosted its rating on DraftKings (NASDAQ:DKNG) to Buy from Hold as it sees the company, along with FanDuel, as one of the winners in mobile sports betting in the U.S. The firm expects DraftKings and FanDuel to maintain over 80% market share.

DuPont de Nemours (NYSE:DD) was upgraded to Overweight from Sector Weight by analysts at KeyBanc Securities, as they pointed to the chemical maker’s strong balance sheet and defensive characteristics amid economic stress.

Redburn upgraded database software provider MongoDB (NASDAQ:MDB) to Neutral from Sell, citing limited downside risk. Analyst Alexander Haissl noted that MongoDB’s valuation multiple is near all-time lows and earnings expectations have already been reset. While macro-related risks persist, Haissl said they are not specific to the company.

data.title: Over 70% of companies deliver EPS wins this week: Earnings Scorecard
data.description:
Wall Street’s major averages ended in red for the holiday-shortened week, driven by a post-earnings slump in industry bellwether UnitedHealth (UNH) and tensions between U.S. President Donald Trump and Federal Reserve chair Jerome Powell.

UnitedHealth (UNH) fell more than 22% on Thursday after delivering a rare quarterly bottom-line miss. The performance reverberated across the health insurance sector, dragging peers such as Humana (HUM) and Molina Healthcare (MOH). 

Fed chair Powell was in the spotlight on Thursday, after Trump criticized him for being too slow to reduce interest rates and said his termination could not "come fast enough." 

Powell on Wednesday contributed to a steep Wall Street selloff after expressing concerns about the likely higher-than-anticipated effects from tariffs. He also said that tariffs could possibly put pressure on policymakers' dual mandate of stable prices and strong employment.

Earnings Roundup: 

With markets shut on Good Friday, investors have got some window to evaluate the effects of tariffs and prepare for an earnings heavy week. Despite disappointing earnings from UnitedHealth (UNH) which dragged the Wall Street, the earnings scorecard for this week was fairly optimistic. 

From the 31 S&P 500 companies that reported earnings this week, 23 of them beat EPS estimates, while 17 of them beat revenue expectations. 

Sector-wise performance: 

Communication Services: Netflix (NFLX) said its first-quarter earnings beat expectations on both top and bottom lines thanks to positive results both in subscriptions and advertising.

Revenue of $10.54B topped analyst consensus for $10.5B, and diluted earnings per share of $6.61 easily topped views for $5.68.

Financial: Citigroup (C)’s Q1 earnings and revenue beat consensus estimates, with strong performance in its markets and investment banking businesses.

The bank kept its 2025 guidance unchanged, subject to macro and market conditions. It expects net interest income, ex-markets, up 2%-3% from 2024. 

Goldman Sachs (GS) Q1 earnings and revenue significantly outperformed Wall Street consensus estimates, reflecting robust increases in the bank's fixed income, currency and commodities revenue as well as equity revenue. A lower provision for credit losses also helped.

Q1 GAAP EPS of $14.12, topped the average analyst estimate of $12.27, and total revenue of $15.06B, beat the $14.7B consensus. 

Bank of America (BAC) turned in bottom-line and top-line beats, helped by sales and trading revenue growth at its Global Markets division.

The bank's year-over-year 18% increase in GAAP EPS "reflected growth in net interest income and fee income, while sales and trading delivered its 12th consecutive quarter of year-over-year revenue growth," said Chairman and CEO Brian Moynihan.

Bank of America expects Q4 net interest income on fully taxable equivalent basis of $15.5B-$15.7B.

Healthcare: Abbott Laboratories (ABT) Q1 2025 results fell short of Street forecasts for revenue, despite an earnings beat amid outperformances in its MedTech and Pharma divisions.

The North Chicago, Illinois-based healthcare giant reaffirmed its earnings outlook amid uncertainty over the Trump administration’s tariffs targeted at MedTech and Pharma products.

Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) Q1 results exceeded Wall Street’s expectations, with its pharmaceutical segment offsetting weakness in its MedTech segment. The company also projected a $400M headwind attributed to tariffs in 2025.

Speaking at JNJ’s earnings call, CFO Joseph Wolk said that much of that impact mainly relates to trade levies that the company’s MedTech division is likely to face after President Donald Trump exempted pharmaceuticals from a raft of tariffs 

Industrials: J.B. Hunt Transport Services (JBHT) topped consensus estimates with its FQ1 earnings report, but the transportation and logistics company reported operating revenue fell 1% to $2.92 billion. 

Operating income decreased 8% year-over-year to $178.7 million for FQ1. The decrease in operating income was primarily due to the decline in DCS revenue, lower yields in JBI, higher insurance claim and premium expense, increased group medical costs, and higher equipment and maintenance expense.

United Airlines (UAL) basic economy fueled a better-than-expected profit of $0.91 per share, swinging from a loss of $0.15 in the same quarter last year and beating Wall Street’s expectations by 17 cents.

Gains have been restrained, however, by the company’s warning that its profitability will be adversely impacted if the economy enters a recession.

Earnings next week:

More than 100 companies are scheduled to report earnings next week, with the likes of Home Bancorp (HBCP) SmartFinancial (SMBK), and Comerica (CMA) reporting on Monday. Tuesday will see earnings reports from Tesla (TSLA), GE Aerospace (GE), Verizon (VZ), and Lockheed Martin (LMT), while Wednesday will include Philip Morris (PM), IBM (IBM), AT&T (T), ServiceNow (NOW), and Boeing (BA). 

Amazon (AMZN), P&G (PG), Merck&Co (MRK), and Intel (INTC) are set to report on Thursday, while companies such as AbbVie (ABBV), Colgate-Palmolive (CL), and Phillips 66 (PSX) will report on Friday.

data.title: Broadcom, Marvell among 'top picks' heading into earnings season: JP Morgan
data.description:
As the cloud of tariffs looms over tech companies entering the earnings season, several semiconductor and semiconductor equipment companies stand out as top picks, according to a preview by J.P. Morgan. 

These include Broadcom (NASDAQ:AVGO), Marvell Technology (NASDAQ:MRVL), Analog Devices (NASDAQ:ADI), KLA (NASDAQ:KLAC) and Synopsys (NASDAQ:SNPS). 

While most first quarter earnings in the sector should meet expectations, tariff and trade related dynamics will drive weaker results during the second half of 2025.

"We expect 1Q earnings season to kick off the negative earnings revision cycle with overall forward 12-month EPS estimates to be potentially cut by ~15-25% over the next two-three earning seasons, which we believe should help the semi stock bottoming process," said J.P. Morgan analysts, led by Harlan Sur, in an extensive investor note. "With stocks having already declined by approximately 25% since the tariff concerns surfaced, we see the potential for further 10-15% downside in stocks over the next few months."

Although tariff and trade related dynamics will result in weaker semiconductor fundamentals, the analysts believe this will be at least partially offset by sustained artificial intelligence and data center demand.

"We believe end-markets with elevated exposure to price-elasticity particularly consumer-facing end products (e.g. PC, smartphone, and consumer appliances) have a higher risk of demand destruction relative to less price sensitive end markets (e.g. server/AI spend)," Sur noted. "On secular trends, we remain constructive on the fundamental setup in the areas of AI cloud/datacenter and custom ASICs."

Looking back to the China and U.S. tariff war from 2018, the average share value in the sector declined by 30% to 35% from the peak to the trough, which took place from spring to December. Earnings per share estimates were cut by a total of 20% to 25% over two of the three quarters.

"Given this backdrop, we now expect semi industry revenue to be flat to up mid single-digit % this year (versus prior view of +10-12% Y/Y) and expect upcoming EPS estimate cuts should, to some degree, lead to some de-risking," Sur said. "In semicap equipment, we expect weaker 2H fundamentals with CY25 WFE potentially being flat to down mid single-digits% Y/Y (versus our prior view of WFE +5% Y/Y)."

data.title: Trump to fast-track permits for 10 mining projects, including Rio Tinto's Arizona copper mine
data.description:
The Trump administration said on Friday it will fast-track permitting for 10 mining projects across the U.S. as part of its push to expand production of critical minerals.

The initial 10 projects - the White House said it plans to add more - include Rio TInto's (NYSE:RIO) proposed Resolute copper mine in Arizona; the proposed Stibnite antimony and gold mine in Idaho from Perpetua Resources (PPTA), Hecla Mining's (HL) proposed Libby copper and silver mine in Montana; the expansion of the Silver Peak lithium mine in Nevada by Albemarle (ALB); an Arkansas direct lithium extraction project from Standard Lithium (SLI); and an Alabama metallurgical coal project from Warrior Met Coal (HCC).

Rio Tinto (NYSE:RIO) said it believes its Resolution copper project is "vital to securing America's energy future and infrastructure needs with a domestic supply of copper."

The projects were granted FAST-41 status through a federal initiative launched in 2015 to streamline approvals of critical infrastructure; South32's (OTCPK:SOUHY) Hermosa zinc and manganese project in Arizona was fast-tracked by former President Biden, the first mine to receive FAST-41 status.

Earlier this week, President Trump ordered a probe that could lead to new tariffs on all U.S. critical minerals imports, many of which currently come from China.

data.title: Tesla said to have delayed U.S. production of cheaper Model Y
data.description:
Electric vehicle maker Tesla's (NASDAQ:TSLA) plans for an affordable U.S.-made version of its best-selling Model Y SUV has been delayed by at least a few months, Reuters reported on Friday, citing three sources with knowledge of the matter.

Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) has publicly said it is on track to launch more affordable cars in H1 2025. However, according to the sources, global production of the cheaper Model Y in the U.S. will now start at least a few months later than the public plan. The sources offered several revised targets ranging from Q3 to early next year. 

According to Reuters, two of the sources said the company is aiming to produce 250K of the cheaper Model Ys in the U.S. in 2026. 

Tesla (TSLA) did not immediately respond to Seeking Alpha's request for comment. 

The Reuters report comes ahead of the electric vehicle maker's Q1 results on April 22. Tesla (TSLA) has had a tough 2025, struggling with shrinking sales due to intense competition, and a wave of backlash against its products triggered by ire at top boss Elon Musk and his actions as a key government advisor to U.S. President Donald Trump.

TSLA stock is -40.2% YTD.

data.title: Fed proposes averaging bank stress test results over two years to reduce volatility
data.description:
The Federal Reserve on Thursday said it had proposed to average major banks' stress test results over two consecutive years to reduce the Y/Y changes in capital requirements.

The proposal is the first in a series of actions by the central bank to implement a major overhaul to its annual evaluation of a lender's ability to weather crises such as a financial crash or a recession. The stress test estimates a bank's losses, revenue and capital levels under such hypothetical severe scenarios.     

"Due to the changing hypothetical nature of the test, the results also change and introduce volatility each year. The results, in part, determine the calibration of the stress capital buffer (SCB), which is one component of the amount of capital large banks must hold to absorb losses," the Fed said in a statement.

The Fed's proposal would also give banks three additional months to adjust their new capital requirements by delaying the annual effective date of the SCB requirement from October 1 to January 1 of the following year. 

Finally, the proposal would make targeted changes to streamline stress test-related data collection. 

"These proposed changes are not designed to materially affect overall capital requirements," the Fed said.

The central bank in December last year unveiled its plans to overhaul the stress tests.

The Fed's announcement was immediately followed by a lawsuit on behalf of a group of major banks representing firms such as JPMorgan (NYSE:JPM), Citi (NYSE:C) and Goldman Sachs (NYSE:GS). The group claimed that the stress tests continued to produce capital charges that were "inaccurate, volatile and excessive, resulting in reduced lending and economic growth."

As per Fed data, here are the top 10 largest commercial U.S. banks ranked by consolidated assets as of December 31, 2024: 

  1. JPMorgan (NYSE:JPM); consol. assets: $3.46T, SA Quant Rating: Hold.
  2. Bank of America (NYSE:BAC); consol. assets: $2.59T, SA Quant Rating: Hold.
  3. Wells Fargo (NYSE:WFC); consol. assets: $1.71T, SA Quant Rating: Strong Buy.
  4. Citi (NYSE:C); consol. assets: $1.70T, SA Quant Rating: Hold.
  5. U.S. Bancorp (NYSE:USB); consol. assets: $662.91B, SA Quant Rating: Hold.
  6. Goldman Sachs (NYSE:GS); consol. assets: $558.24B, SA Quant Rating: Hold.
  7. PNC Financial Services (NYSE:PNC); consol. assets: $556.14B, SA Quant Rating: Hold.
  8. Truist Financial (NYSE:TFC); consol. assets: $523.13B, SA Quant Rating: Hold. 
  9. Capital One Financial (NYSE:COF); consol. assets: $487.19B, SA Quant Rating: Strong Buy. 
  10. TD Bank (NYSE:TD); consol. assets: $372.78B, SA Quant Rating: Hold. 

Here are some exchange-traded funds that track the Financial sector: (NYSEARCA:XLF), (NYSEARCA:VFH), (NYSEARCA:IYF), (NYSEARCA:FNCL), (NYSEARCA:IYG), and (NYSEARCA:FXO).

data.title: U.S. government COVID website replaced with China 'lab leak' theory
data.description:
The U.S. federal government's "covid.gov" website now redirects to a White House page offering up the unproven theory that the COVID-19 virus originated and was leaked from a Chinese laboratory.

The website earlier used to house information on the COVID-19 pandemic including facts on vaccines, treatment and testing. It was an official website of the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services.

The website now takes the user to a "whitehouse.gov" address featuring a full-length image of U.S. President Donald Trump at the top along with the words "Lab Leak: The True Origins of COVID-19."

The website takes aim at the pandemic policies instituted by former President Joe Biden, and also criticized Biden's former chief medical officer Anthony Fauci for pushing what it called the "preferred narrative that COVID-19 originated naturally."

The "lab leak" theory was offered up by a U.S. House of Representatives subcommittee led by Rep. Brad Wenstrup (R-OH) in December 2024. 

The revamp of the COVID website comes amid numerous such changes and edits made to federal government web pages since Trump took office, reflecting the agenda of his administration.

Here are some exchange-traded funds tracking the Health Care sector: (NYSEARCA:XLV), (VHT), (IHI), (IXJ), (IYH), (FHLC), and (FXH).

And here are some stocks of COVID-19 vaccine makers: Moderna (MRNA), Pfizer (PFE), BioNTech (BNTX), Novavax (NVAX), Valneva (VALN), AstraZeneca (AZN) and Johnson & Johnson (JNJ).   

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Ford said to have halted shipments of F-150s and other models to China
data.description:
U.S. carmaker Ford (NYSE:F) has stopped shipments of some of its vehicle models, including its best-selling F-150 pickup truck, to China in order to avoid tariffs, the Wall Street Journal reported on Friday, citing people familiar with the matter.

Ford (NYSE:F) halted shipments this week of F-150 Raptors, Mustang muscle cars and Bronco SUVs built in Mich., as well as Lincoln Navigators made in Ky., WSJ said, citing the people. 

According to the people, the U.S. and China's retaliatory trade barriers against each other have resulted in tariffs on those vehicles rising as high as 150%. The F-150 Raptor, for example, sells for nearly $100K in China.

Ford (F) did not immediately respond to Seeking Alpha's request for comment. 

Two days ago, Automotive News reported that Ford (F) had told its dealers it might have to increase prices for consumers next month to offset the cost of tariffs. 

Of the major "Detroit Three" carmakers, Ford (F) has the least exposure to U.S. trade policy, with more cars made in the U.S. than rivals General Motors (GM) and Stellantis (STLA).

data.title: Capital One and Discover Financial's $35B merger gets final regulatory nods
data.description:
Capital One Financial (NYSE:COF) and Discover Financial Services (NYSE:DFS) on Friday said they had got the final regulatory approvals for their $35B merger, paving the way for what would become the eight-largest bank in the U.S.

The Federal Reserve Board and the Office of the Comptroller of the Currency (OCC) approved the deal. 

"The OCC conducted a fulsome review of the application submitted March 21, 2024, to ensure all statutory and regulatory requirements have been met," the agency said in a statement. "Today’s announcement reflects the OCC’s careful analysis of the effect of the merger on communities, the banking industry, and the U.S. financial system," it added. 

The Fed Board also entered into a consent order with Discover Financial (NYSE:DFS) and assessed the lender a fine of $100M for "overcharging certain interchange fees from 2007 through 2023." DFS has "since terminated these practices and is repaying those fees to affected customers," the Board said in a statement.

According to the Fed, the combined entity post the merger would become the eight largest U.S. insured depository organization, with consolidated assets of around $637.8B. That figure would represent 2.2% of the country's total insured deposits.

"There will be no immediate changes to Capital One and Discover customer accounts and relationships now or in the period immediately following the closing of the transaction," the banks said in a joint statement.

The merger is expected to close on May 18.

data.title: MetroCity Bankshares GAAP EPS of $0.63 beats by $0.02, revenue of $36.01M beats by $0.91M
data.description:
• MetroCity Bankshares press release (NASDAQ:MCBS): Q1 GAAP EPS of $0.63 beats by $0.02.
  • Revenue of $36.01M (+10.3% Y/Y) beats by $0.91M.
